summary:
The Hephthalite Empire (c. 420–560 AD), also called the White Huns, was a Central Asian nomadic power that emerged in Bactria (modern Afghanistan and Central Asia) and for over a century dominated a vast territory from Persia to northwestern India, dealing devastating blows to both the [[event:2059|Sassanid Empire]] and the [[event:83|Gupta Empire]]. Their ethnic identity and origin remain debated — Chinese, Byzantine, and Indian sources all describe them differently, and their relationship to the Hunnic peoples of the west is disputed. Unlike the Huns of Europe under Attila, the Hephthalites were settled or semi-settled, controlled cities and trade routes, and used their own script.

In India, Hephthalite (locally called 'Hunas') invasions beginning around 460–470 AD struck the Gupta Empire repeatedly, killing the Gupta emperor Skandagupta in battle and fatally weakening the empire that had produced India's [[event:2053|Golden Age]]. The Hephthalites destroyed the [[event:2051|Gandhara Buddhist art]] centers and reportedly razed thousands of Buddhist monasteries, dealing a catastrophic blow to Buddhist civilization in the northwest. In the west, they defeated and killed the Sassanid emperor Peroz I in 484 AD, forcing Persia to pay tribute. The Hephthalite Empire was eventually destroyed in 560 AD by a coordinated Sassanid-Turkic alliance, with the Sassanid Emperor Khosrow I defeating them from the west and the Göktürk Khaganate from the east, dividing their territory between the two powers.
